content_en:
Sheikh al-Fawzan dedicates this chapter to the vast domain of Islamic commercial law — buy-and-sell contracts, financial dealings, and economic transactions. He begins with the Quranic principle that Allah has made trade lawful and interest forbidden (2:275), establishing the ethical foundation of Islamic finance: wealth generation through genuine exchange of value is encouraged, while exploitation through usury is categorically prohibited.

The conditions for a valid sale contract are enumerated: the buyer and seller must be legally competent, the offer and acceptance must be clearly expressed, the object of sale must be known, must exist, must belong to the seller, and must be deliverable. Sheikh al-Fawzan discusses the types of option (khiyar) available to the contracting parties — the option of the session (khiyar al-majlis), the stipulated option (khiyar al-shart), and the option due to defect (khiyar al-ayb) — explaining how these mechanisms protect the parties from injustice and deception.

Riba (usury/interest) receives extensive treatment. The author distinguishes between riba al-fadl (inequality in the exchange of ribawi commodities of the same type) and riba al-nasi'ah (delay in exchange), citing the famous hadith specifying the six ribawi commodities — gold, silver, wheat, barley, dates, and salt. He explains how contemporary fiat currency is treated analogously to gold and silver, making interest on loans and bank deposits prohibited.

Forbidden contracts are catalogued: sales involving gharar (excessive uncertainty), the sale of what one does not own, conditional sales with multiple options combined in a single contract, and other forms identified by classical jurists and supported by hadith evidence. The chapter also covers legitimate complex transactions: salam (advance purchase), ijarah (rental), shirkah (partnership), mudarabah (profit-sharing), and waqf (endowment), providing the legal framework that underpins Islamic economic activity. Contemporary applications such as takaful (Islamic insurance) are mentioned with reference to their scholarly treatment. Sheikh al-Fawzan's inclusion of contemporary financial questions alongside classical Hanbali fiqh reflects his understanding that the law must remain practically applicable — that fiqh is not a museum of medieval arrangements but a living system capable of generating guidance for each generation. This practical orientation, combined with the work's grounding in primary textual evidence, makes Al-Mulakhas al-Fiqhi one of the most useful single-volume introductions to Hanbali commercial law available to contemporary students and practitioners.
title_id: Jual Beli dan Mu'amalat — Transaksi Finansial
content_id:
Bab mu'amalat (transaksi finansial) dalam Al-Mulakhas al-Fiqhi menyajikan prinsip-prinsip utama fikih ekonomi Islam secara ringkas namun komprehensif. Syekh al-Fawzan membuka dengan dasar-dasar jual beli yang sah, kemudian membahas berbagai jenis transaksi yang diatur syariat.

Pembahasan meliputi: syarat-syarat jual beli yang sah (ijab-qabul, subjek transaksi, penjual dan pembeli), jual beli yang dilarang (riba, gharar/ketidakpastian, jual beli barang yang tidak dimiliki), riba dan dua jenisnya (fadhl dan nasi'ah), salam (jual beli pesanan dengan pembayaran di muka), ijarah (sewa-menyewa dan pengupahan), mudharabah (bagi hasil), musyarakah (kerjasama usaha), qardh (pinjaman), rahn (gadai), hiwalah (pengalihan hutang), wakalah (perwakilan), syuf'ah (hak beli lebih dulu), waqaf, hibah, wasiat, dan perlindungan hak milik dalam Islam.
